task: I’m putting together a punch list for a rehab unit, and I want a simple way to make sure nothing gets signed off too early. What checkpoints would you use so I can catch defects, missing paperwork, or scope creep before I pay anyone?

turns.agent_response:
Use **three gates before sign-off**: **scope complete, quality verified, paperwork complete**.

## 1) Scope complete
Don’t sign off until the work matches the agreed scope.

Check:
- Every line item in the bid is done
- Any approved change orders are documented
- No “extra” work snuck in without approval
- All fixtures/materials installed match what was specified

Ask:
- “Is anything left unfinished?”
- “Did any work get added that wasn’t approved?”
- “Does the finished work match the scope exactly?”

## 2) Quality verified
Inspect the actual work, not just the appearance.

Check:
- Paint coverage / trim / caulk
- Doors, locks, windows, hardware
- Plumbing leaks, drainage, water pressure
- Electrical outlets, switches, fixtures
- HVAC operation
- Appliances functioning
- Floors, tile, grout, alignment
- Signs of water intrusion, cracks, or patchwork
- Cleanliness and final debris removal

Ask:
- “What fails under normal use?”
- “What needs a return visit?”
- “What was hidden behind the finished surface?”

## 3) Paperwork complete
No final payment without the documents.

Check:
- Final invoice
- Lien waivers
- Permit sign-off / final inspection approval if applicable
- Warranty info
- Receipts for major materials
- Change-order approvals
- Before/after photos if you want a record

Ask:
- “Are all lien releases in hand?”
- “Are permits closed out?”
- “Do we have written warranty coverage?”
- “Are all change orders approved and attached?”

## Simple rule
**No paperwork, no payment.  
No inspection pass, no payment.  
No approved scope, no payment.**

## Best workflow
1. Walk the unit
2. Mark punch items
3. Confirm each item is completed
4. Re-inspect
5. Collect paperwork
6. Release payment only after all three gates pass

If you want, I can turn this into a **one-page punch list template** with columns for item, status, photo, and payment hold.

user_simulator.text:
Close, but I’d tighten it up. Three gates is right. I’d want one more layer on the money side so nobody plays games with partial completion.

Use this order:

1. Scope match
- every bid line done
- every change order in writing
- nothing extra billed without approval
- materials match spec

2. Functional check
- plumbing, electrical, HVAC, appliances all work under normal use
- doors/windows/locks operate cleanly
- no leaks, soft spots, patch-throughs, bad caulk, or sloppy finish work
- final clean, trash out, no punch items hidden by cosmetics

3. Paper trail
- final invoice
- lien waivers from everyone who touched the job
- permit final / sign-off if required
- warranty info
- receipts for big-ticket materials
- signed change orders

4. Payment control
- hold back retention until final walkthrough passes
- no check until every lien release is in hand
- if anything is incomplete, write the holdback reason on the punch list and date it

What I’d add: take photos on the walkthrough and note who said what. Last time I had a contractor try to treat “almost done” like done, the paper trail is what kept it from turning into a mess.

If you want this useful, I’d rather have a real punch-list template with columns for: item, location, trade, status, photo, due date, holdback amount, and sign-off initials. That’s the part that actually stops scope creep.

turns.agent_response:
Use this **4-gate closeout**:

## 1) Scope match
- Every bid line completed
- Every change order in writing
- Nothing extra billed without approval
- Materials/fixtures match spec

## 2) Functional check
- Plumbing, electrical, HVAC, appliances work under normal use
- Doors, windows, locks operate cleanly
- No leaks, soft spots, patch-throughs, bad caulk, or sloppy finish work
- Final clean, trash out, no punch items hidden by cosmetics

## 3) Paper trail
- Final invoice
- Lien waivers from everyone who touched the job
- Permit final / sign-off if required
- Warranty info
- Receipts for big-ticket materials
- Signed change orders

## 4) Payment control
- Hold back retention until final walkthrough passes
- No check until every lien release is in hand
- If anything is incomplete, write the holdback reason on the punch list and date it

### Simple rule
**If it isn’t on the punch list, it isn’t done.**  
**If it isn’t documented, it isn’t approved.**  
**If liens aren’t released, money stays put.**

### Best punch-list columns
- Item
- Location
- Trade
- Status
- Photo
- Due date
- Holdback amount
- Sign-off initials

If you want, I can turn this into a **one-page punch list template** you can copy into a spreadsheet.

user_simulator.text:
Better. Still a little soft on the money control.

I’d tighten Gate 4 so it’s not just “holdback until walkthrough” — that’s how people game partial completion and try to slide past missing items.

I’d want it as:

4. Payment control
- retainage held until final completion, not “mostly done”
- no release until punch list is closed in writing
- no final check until all lien waivers are in hand
- no partial billing on unfinished scope unless I approved it in writing
- any incomplete item gets a dollar holdback attached to it, not just a note

And I’d add one line under scope match:
- no substitution of materials or finishes without written approval

That’s the part that catches the cheap swaps.

If you want this actually usable, the punch list template should have a column for **holdback amount per item** and **who approved the release**. That’s what stops the contractor from calling something done when it’s just cosmetically passable.

turns.agent_response:
Use this **5-part closeout**:

## 1) Scope match
- Every bid line completed
- Every change order in writing
- Nothing extra billed without approval
- Materials/fixtures match spec
- **No substitutions of materials or finishes without written approval**

## 2) Functional check
- Plumbing, electrical, HVAC, appliances work under normal use
- Doors, windows, locks operate cleanly
- No leaks, soft spots, patch-throughs, bad caulk, or sloppy finish work
- Final clean, trash out, no punch items hidden by cosmetics

## 3) Paper trail
- Final invoice
- Lien waivers from everyone who touched the job
- Permit final / sign-off if required
- Warranty info
- Receipts for big-ticket materials
- Signed change orders

## 4) Payment control
- Retainage held until **final completion**, not “mostly done”
- No release until punch list is **closed in writing**
- No final check until **all lien waivers** are in hand
- No partial billing on unfinished scope unless **you approved it in writing**
- Any incomplete item gets a **dollar holdback attached to it**, not just a note

## 5) Closeout record
- Photos from final walkthrough
- Date-stamped punch list
- Who approved what
- Who signed off on release of funds

### Simple rule
- **If it isn’t on the punch list, it isn’t done.**
- **If it isn’t documented, it isn’t approved.**
- **If liens aren’t released, money stays put.**

### Punch list columns
- Item
- Location
- Trade
- Status
- Photo
- Due date
- Holdback amount
- Approved by
- Release sign-off

If you want, I can turn this into a **copy-paste spreadsheet template**.

user_simulator.text:
That’s the right shape. Close enough to use.

I’d keep the 5-part closeout and make the money rule even less negotiable:
- no partial pay unless the partial scope is separately completed, priced, and approved in writing
- no “substantial completion” language unless I’ve already documented what stays open
- every open item gets a dollar value tied to it, not a vague promise

And yes, the closeout record matters. Photos, dates, initials, who approved what — that’s what stops the rewrite later.

The spreadsheet columns you listed are the ones I’d use. If I were tightening it one more notch, I’d add:
- contract line reference
- retainage at risk
- waiver received Y/N
- re-inspection date

That gives you leverage if somebody starts playing cute with the last 5%.
